ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef goulash; 'beef stew' (FOODON_03302757) is the closest defensible match. Goulash is a paprika-spiced Hungarian variant of beef stew.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef stew — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for beef goulash; goulash is a paprika-spiced Central European beef stew preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for beef goulash. A0CKP (beef stew) is the closest defensible code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef goulash or beef stew as a food item in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of beef goulash is approximately 250 g (one bowl), representing a standard main-course portion as commonly served in Singapore hawker or restaurant settings.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Goulash — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Beef Goulash (cr_id F06040003). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on beef stew / low-carbohydrate mixed meat dish category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef goulash in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate mixed beef stew dishes. Carbohydrate content is 2.48 g/100 g, predominantly from vegetables (tomatoes, bell peppers, onions) with no starchy thickener reported. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 2.48 × 2.5) / 100 = 1.86, rounded to 1.9. This is a very low GL consistent with a protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate stew.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 226 mg/100 g — borderline elevated; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 565 mg sodium, representing ~25% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request low-sodium stock during preparation, and avoid adding salt at the table to keep cumulative daily sodium within the 2,300 mg WHO target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.48 g/100 g) and estimated GI of 30 produce a negligible glycemic load of ~1.9 per serving, with high protein (13.2 g/100 g) supporting postprandial glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef goulash is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served without starchy accompaniments; pair with non-starchy vegetables or low-GI sides such as barley to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (beef) contributes saturated fat and haem iron; sodium at 226 mg/100 g adds cardiovascular load; however, total fat is moderate at 4.7 g/100 g and the vegetable components (tomatoes, bell peppers) provide lycopene and antioxidants.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit beef goulash to occasional consumption (once weekly), use lean beef cuts, reduce added salt, and ensure the dish is part of a diet otherwise low in saturated fat and sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid, a precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids relevant to RA pathophysiology; nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, bell peppers, paprika) may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit beef goulash; consider substituting beef with turkey or legumes and replacing nightshade vegetables with courgette or carrot if nightshade sensitivity is confirmed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef goulash is a hearty Hungarian stew originating in Central Europe, made by slow-cooking tender chunks of beef (typically chuck or shin) with sweet and smoked paprika, onions, tomatoes, and bell peppers in a rich, deeply coloured broth. The dish is characterised by its bold, smoky-sweet paprika flavour, tender fall-apart beef, and a thick, slightly tangy sauce derived from the tomatoes and long cooking time. Nutritionally, beef goulash is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish: per 100 g it provides 109 kcal, 13.2 g protein, 4.7 g fat, and only 2.48 g carbohydrate, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is 226 mg/100 g, which is borderline elevated and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (~30) and the glycemic load per standard 250 g serving is approximately 1.9, making it an excellent choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when served without starchy sides. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free in its traditional preparation. It is widely available in Singapore restaurants and hawker-adjacent eateries serving European or fusion cuisine.

How much sodium is in beef goulash?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and secondary nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id F06040003) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from a primary approved regional source. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI component only) documented with full reasoning.
notes: Fields set to -1 due to data unavailability in the HPB Foodo source for this record: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for Beef Goulash as of 2026-04-16. The input record referenced a trans fat value of 0.1 g/100 g in a health caution note, but this was not present in the structured per_100g data and is not confirmed by the HPB source; it has not been included. GI is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate mixed beef stew category from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for beef goulash was found. Halal dietary flag is set based on the input dietary_tags; kosher status is not confirmed. The dish is of Hungarian origin and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the G6 multilingual rules, which apply to Singaporean dishes only. A Hungarian romanized local name (Gulyás) is provided as cultural context.
